coredata.dc:title: One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for copper ion assay and multicolor cell imaging using fluorescence quenchometric method
coredata.dc:creator: Meng, Alan
Huangfu, Baihe
Sheng, Liying
Hong, Xiaocheng
Li, Zhenjiang
coredata.dc:description: Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) with a high quantum yield (QY, 25.3%) and strong fluorescence (FL) performance is prepared through one-pot hydrothermal method using folic acid as the source of nitrogen and carbon, glycerol as the source of carbon, and borax as the source of boron. B,N-CDs has uniform dispersion with the average size of 5.1 ± 0.8 nm. The fluorescence of B,N-CDs is gradually quenched upon sequential addition of copper ion (Cu2+). And a linear relationship is observed in the range of 0.005–5 μM with a detection limit of 1.6 nM. The unique quenching of fluorescence (measured at excitation/emission wavelengths of 365/442 nm) is attributed to the selective capture of Cu2+ by the amino and amide groups on the surface of B,N-CDs. The amino group on the surface of B,N-CDs complex with Cu2+ to split the d orbital of Cu2+. This causes the excited state electrons from B,N-CDs transfer to the d orbital of Cu2+ and quenched the fluorescence. B,N-CDs display satisfactory stability, anti-interference performance and low cytotoxicity performance. It is successfully applied to the determination of Cu2+ in actual water samples. B,N-CDs treated cells display distinguished blue and green colors under a confocal microscope.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent nanoprobe
Copper ion determination
Low cytotoxicity
Quenching mechanism
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties and stability of B,N-CDs
Effect of pH on fluorescence assay system

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The work reported here was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 52072196, 52002199, 52002200, Major Basic Research Program of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2020ZD09, the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2019BEM042, the Innovation and Technology Program of Shandong Province under Grant No. 2020KJA004, the Open Project of the Chemistry Department in Qingdao University of Science and Technology of China (Grant No. QUSTHX201813), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2020 M683450) and the Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province under No. ts201511034. We express our grateful thanks to them for their financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FL intensity of B,N-CDs under different carbonization temperature (A), time (B) and solvent medium (C).
(A) TEM image of B,N-CDs(inset: upper left, HRTEM of B,N-CDs; lower left: EDS of B,N-CDs). (B)size distribution of B,N-CDs (detect 30 particles). (C)Surface energy spectrum of B, N, C, O elements.
(A) The full scan XPS spectra of B,N-CDs, high- resolution spectra of (B) C1s, (C) N1s, (D) B1s, and (E) O1s.

(A) UV–vis spectrum of B,N-CDs, inset: the photograph of B,N-CDs under natural light (right) and UV light at 365 nm wavelength (left), (B) Fluorescent emission spectra of B,N-CDs under different excitation wavelengths, (C) Fluorescence lifetime decay curve of B,N-CDs.

(A) fluorescence intensity of B,N-CDs in the presence and absence of Cu2+ (0.03 M), (B) fluorescence emission spectra of B,N-CDs after adding various concentrations of Cu2+ (from top to bottom: 0, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.05, 0.1, 0.5, 1, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 200, 400, 600, 800, 1000 μM), (C) plots of intensity ratio (F0/F) with the change of Cu2+ concentrations (n = 3), (D) The Stern-Volmer plot of B,N-CDs.

(A) the selectivity of B,N-CDs towards various metal ions with the concentration of 10−3 M (Fe2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Zn2+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, K+, Na+ and Cu2+, n = 3); (B) the anti-interference performance towards Cu2+, the concentration of all metal ions is 10−3 M (n = 3).
Confocal fluorescence microscopic images of Hela cells after incubation with B,N-CDs at (A) bright field, and at excitation wavelengths of (B) 405 nm, (C) 488 nm.
Schematic illustration of the process for preparing B,N-CDs, assaying copper ion and cell imaging.

head.ce:title: One-pot hydrothermal synthesis of boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots for copper ion assay and multicolor cell imaging using fluorescence quenchometric method

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optic-electric Sensing and Analytical Chemistry for Life Science
College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering
Qingdao University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) with high quantum yield (25.3%) and strong fluorescence performance is used for Cu2+ determination and multicolor cell imaging. It exhibits wide linear range, low detection limit, satisfactory stability, selectivity and low cytotoxicity performance.

Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B,N-CDs) with a high quantum yield (QY, 25.3%) and strong fluorescence (FL) performance is prepared through one-pot hydrothermal method using folic acid as the source of nitrogen and carbon, glycerol as the source of carbon, and borax as the source of boron. B,N-CDs has uniform dispersion with the average size of 5.1 ± 0.8 nm. The fluorescence of B,N-CDs is gradually quenched upon sequential addition of copper ion (Cu2+). And a linear relationship is observed in the range of 0.005–5 μM with a detection limit of 1.6 nM. The unique quenching of fluorescence (measured at excitation/emission wavelengths of 365/442 nm) is attributed to the selective capture of Cu2+ by the amino and amide groups on the surface of B,N-CDs. The amino group on the surface of B,N-CDs complex with Cu2+ to split the d orbital of Cu2+. This causes the excited state electrons from B,N-CDs transfer to the d orbital of Cu2+ and quenched the fluorescence. B,N-CDs display satisfactory stability, anti-interference performance and low cytotoxicity performance. It is successfully applied to the determination of Cu2+ in actual water samples. B,N-CDs treated cells display distinguished blue and green colors under a confocal microscope.

ce:section.ce:para: As a heavy metal ion, copper ion (Cu2+) is also an essential trace element for living organism. A small amount of Cu2+ can promote the growth of animals and plants, but when a certain amount of Cu2+ is accumulated in the body, the symptoms of damage will occur. In the water environment, when the Cu2+ concentration is greater than 8 μg·L−1, it can inhibit some enzymes in the digestive tract, and severely cause liver tissue necrosis [1,2]. Adults can adsorb elements directly from the water through the skin. Besides, the World Health Organization (WHO) stipulated that the indicator of Cu2+ in drinking water is 1 mg·L−1. Hence, it is necessary to assay Cu2+ in various water bodies accurately. In view of this, a host of analytical methods have been taken to trace Cu2+ in multifarious samples, involving the graphite furnace atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS), flow injection analysis (FIA), and electrochemical sensors [3], colorimetric sensors [4], fluorescent probes [5-9], enzymatic assay [10] and so on. In particular, the fluorescent probes have been widely used for Cu2+ quantitation due to the high sensitivity and good selectivity. Fluorescent probes mainly include traditional organic fluorescent materials [7,8], semiconductor materials and some emerging materials [6]. However, traditional fluorescent probes often have disadvantages such as high toxicity and complicated synthesis process. In short, it is necessary to develop new fluorescent materials with superior performance and greener synthesis methods for Cu2+ assay (Scheme 1 ).

Carbon dots, as a newly emerging fluorescent nanomaterial, have received extensive attention. CDs have been widely used in bioimaging, luminescent probe analysis, pharmacology, environmental science and other fields [11-14] due to their small molecular weight, high photostability, low toxicity, water solubility and biocompatibility and adjustable emission wavelength. Carbon dot based fluorescent probes have been applied to assay multifarious heavy metal ions. Li et al. developed a nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped carbon dots by microwave-assisted thermolysis of N-phosphonomethyl aminodiacetic acid and ethylenediamine with the quantum yield of 17.5% [15]. Wang et al. developed a carbon dot (QY = 4.84%) for the determination of Cu2+ by carbonizing poly-ethylenimine (PEI) under microwave irradiation [16]. Huang et al. proposed nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles using one-pot hydrothermal treatment of strawberry juice for determination Hg2+ with a linear range from 10 nM to 50 μM [17]. Shen et al. reported a kind of N, S-CDs (QY = 18.7%), and applied it in Cr (VI) assay. It showed good linear relationship as the concentration of Cr (VI) change from 1 to 40 μM [18]. Wang et al. reported the synthesis of boron-doped carbon nanodots (B-C-dots) by a hydrothermal method and applied it for determination Pb (II), Cu (II) and pyrophosphate ions [19]. Liu et al. prepared a kind of B,N,S-CDs (QY = 5.44%) with red emission for the dual mode (colorimetric and fluorescence) determination of Fe (III) with the detection limit at 90 nM [20]. Zhao et al. developed a CD-based nanohybrid dual-emission system as a ratiometric sensor and applied it in Hg2+ sensing with a limit of 12 ng mL−1 [21]. Although some good results have been achieved in carbon dot based fluorescent materials, the QY and detection limit towards heavy metal ions need to be further improved. Therefore, it is still a challenging and meaningful work to obtain CDs with higher QY, lower detection limit by doping.

Herein, based on the hydrothermal method, a highly QY B,N-CDs was constructed as a novel ion nanoprobe to assay copper ions using folic acid (FA) and glycerol as C sources, and borax as B source. Moreover, B,N-CDs were found to have highly selective and sensitive towards Cu2+. More important, the results indicate that B,N-CDs shows good performance in actual simple assay. Simultaneously, it possessed good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity towards Hela cells in cell imaging. This work not only obtained B, N-CDs by one-pot synthesis method for assaying copper ions and Hela cell imaging, but also provided a reference for the synthesis of other hetero-doped CDs with high-performance.
Reagents and apparatus required for the experiments are detailed in Supporting Information.

B,N-CDs were synthesized directly via one-pot hydrothermal method. Briefly, 0.5 g FA, 0.1 g borax and 1 mL glycerol were dissolved into 30.0 mL DI water, and stirred magnetically for 10 min using magnetic stirrer. Subsequently, the above solution was transferred into a conventional Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave (Capacity of 50 mL), and heated to 160 °C for 10 h in a hot air oven. After the reaction, the obtained solution was cooled down naturally to room temperature and centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 15 min in order to remove the larger particles. The supernatant after centrifugation was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter, and further transferred into a dialysis bag (1000 Da) to remove the unreacted materials. Finally, a clear yellow solution was obtained for further application.

The FL spectra of each sample were recorded on a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-4600, Hitachi, Japan) with the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. Plus, the slits were 5 nm whether in excitation or emission. Quinine sulfate (QS), whose QY is ∼ 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4 at 365 nm, was chosen as a reference. The QY (Ф) of B,N-CDs was calculated by the following formula.
(1)
Φ CDs = Φ R
ICD s/IR
AR / ACD s
η CDs / η R
2
where ‘Ф’ is the quantum yield, ‘I’ stands for the FL intensity, ‘A’ represents the absorbance and ‘η’ refer to the refractive index of the solvent used. The subscript ‘CDs’ and ‘R’ symbolize B,N-CDs and the reference fluorophore of known.

The determination of Cu2+ was performed at room temperature in deionized water. All the B,N-CD solutions here were used after diluting 10 times with deionized water. Briefly, a certain concentration range of Cu2+ (900 μL, 0–0.001 M) was added to B,N-CD solutions (300 μL), respectively, and then oscillated to make the mixture homogenize to assess the sensitivity. Other different metal cations (Ag+, Al3+, Cr3+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, K+, Mg2+, Na+) with the same concentration (10−3 M) were operated in the same way to verify the selectivity of B,N-CDs. To analyze the anti-interference performance of B,N-CDs as fluorescent probes, a series of experiments were conducted by mixing B,N-CDs, other metal ions with the same concentration (10−3 M) and Cu2+ (10−3 M) together, respectively. FL spectra were measured at 365 nm, and the slits were 5 nm whether in excitation or emission.

MTT (3-(4,5-Dimethyl-2-2thiazolyl)-2,5-diphenyl-2H-tetrazoliumbromide) assay was adopted to assess the cytotoxicity of B,N-CDs. Firstly, Hela cells (5 × 104 per well) were seeded into 96-well plates, and cultured in incubator (5% CO2, 37 °C, 24 h) using Dulbecco's Modified Eagle's as medium (DMEM). The B,N-CDs were diluted 10 times and 5 times and named as sample 2 and sample 1, respectively. The raw B, N-CD solutions was named as sample 3. These samples were added to the cells in each well for further incubation of 24 h. MTT reagent (5 mg·mL−1, 20 μL) were added and incubated continuously with different samples for another 4 h towards each well. After that, dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, 150 μL) was added to each well containing the above-mentioned added substance, and the mixture were placed on a shaker shock for 10 min (protected from light). The optical density (OD) of the above mixture were captured by an enzyme-linked immunosorbent detector at 490 nm and 570 nm, respectively. The cell viability was calculated on the basis of the formula in the following:
(2)
cellviability
( % )
=
O
D
treated
O
D
control
× 100 %

After the Hela cells are cultured for 24 h alone, 300 μL B,N-CDs were added into the Hela cells and incubated for another 24 h. And the mixture was rinsed with phosphate buffer solution (pH = 7.4) to remove the remaining B,N-CDs, and then the cells were fixed on a microscope slide with glycerol. The confocal FL images were recorded by a Leica CTS SP8 confocal scanning microscope (Excited at 422, 502, 488 nm respectively).

B,N-CDs with the best FL performance were acquired by optimizing the reaction conditions including reaction time, temperature, solvent as well as proportion of the different reaction materials. As displayed in Fig. 1 A, the FL intensity increased as the extension of the carbonized temperature changed from 140 to 160 °C, and then decreased as the extension of the carbonized temperature reached 180 °C. Hence, 160 °C was selected the optimal temperature in the subsequent studies. The FL intensity of B,N-CDs increased with the extension of the carbonized time until 10 h, and then decreased with extension of carbonized time (Fig. 1B). Therefore, 10 h was chosen as an optimal reaction time in the further experiments. Moreover, the FL intensity of B,N-CDs prepared in water was stronger than that in ethanol (Fig. 1C). The proportion of the different reaction materials was obtained by changing the quality of the three reaction materials (Table S1). The optimal addition amount of the three raw materials was 0.5 g for FA, 0.1 g for borax and 1 mL for glycerol. Hence, B,N-CDs used in the subsequent experiments were all prepared under the aforementioned optimized conditions.

The morphological characteristics and microstructure of the as-prepared sample were obtained by the typical TEM image [22]. It revealed that B,N-CDs were monodispersed particles with spherical shape (Fig. 2 A). HRTEM image (the inset of Fig. 2A) showed a clear lattice spacing distance of 0.30 nm, and this responds to the (002) facet of graphitic carbon [23,24]. The corresponding size distribution histogram was plotted in Fig. 2B. Apparently, the majority particles size distributed range from 3.0 to 7.0 nm. The mean diameters of B,N-CDs were 5.1 ± 0.8 nm. Energy-dispersive X-ray analysis (Fig. 2A inset) apparently manifest the existence of B, N, C, and O in the products. The elemental mapping images were presented in Fig. 2C. It can be obviously seen that the elements B, N, C, and O were distributed evenly across B,N-CDs. B, N, C were from B,N-CDs, and O may be from the functional groups exist on the surface of B,N-CDs.

To investigate the types of functional groups existed on the surface of B,N-CDs, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was performed and the results was shown in Fig. S1A. The results proved that there is indeed B, N co-doped CDs, and the doping of B, N made a large number of functional groups exist on the surface of B,N-CDs, which improve the QY. The detailed description about FT-IR of B,N-CDs was presented in Supporting Information. As shown in XRD pattern (Fig. S1B), a broad diffraction peak centered at around 26.0° could be attributed to (002) plane, indicating B,N-CDs was graphitic structure [25] . The Raman spectrum (Fig. S1C) of the synthesized B,N-CDs exhibits a D band (sp3 hybridized) at 1355.7 cm−1 and a G band (sp2 hybridized) at 1597.7 cm−1. The D and G bands are associated with the disordered structures and graphitic structures, respectively [25] .

To further demonstrate the chemical composition of the obtained sample, XPS testing was employed [26]. The full scan XPS spectrum of B,N-CDs (Fig. 3 A) revealed the peaks at 284.8, 399.7, 192.0 and 531.5 eV. They were corresponding to C 1 s, N 1 s, B 1 s and O 1 s, respectively. And the atomic percent of C, N, B and O were about 49.3%, 7.3%, 4.3%, and 38.5%, respectively. Fig. 3B represented the high-resolution of C 1 s spectra. Apparently, it displayed five different peaks at 284.5, 286.6, 288.0, 283.5 and 285.2 eV, respectively. The peak at near 284.5 eV indicated the presence of C = C / C–C in B,N-CDs [27,28] . The peak at around 286.6 eV can be attributed to sp3-bonded carbon with oxygen (C-O) [28] . The peaks at 288.0, 283.5 and 285.2 eV can be assigned to CO, C–B, C–H/C–N bonds, respectively [27] . The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3C) displayed two peaks at 400.3 and 399.5 eV. They can be assigned to the pyrrolic N (C–N–C) and amino N (N–H) bonds [18], respectively, proving the successfully doped of N and the presence of amino functional groups. The signal of B-O bond was appeared at 192.1 eV, while the peak at near 191.2 eV indicated the presence of C-B (Fig. 3D) [18,27,28], indicating the successful doping of B element. In addition, The O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 3E) was fitted with three peaks at 532.5, 531.6 and 531.0 eV. They belong to C–OH, C–O/N–O and CO, respectively [29]. The aforementioned analysis results were consistent with that of FT-IR, and proved that B,N-CDs containing a large number of functional groups were obtained successfully.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties and stability of B,N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the characteristic optical properties of B,N-CDs, the UV–vis absorption, fluorescent excitation and emission, FL lifetime were systematically investigated, and results were plotted in Fig. 4 . As can be seen from Fig. 4A inset that B,N-CDs exhibit a bright blue color under the UV illumination at 365 nm (left) and colorless under natural light (right) illumination, indicating B,N-CDs possess strong fluorescent performance. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 4A) includes three characteristic absorption peaks at around 237 nm, 280 nm and 346 nm, which is related to the electron transitions from π (or n) to π* of aromatic sp2 domains (CC) and aromatic heterocycle (CN and CO), respectively [20,30,31]. The emission spectra of B,N-CDs under different excitation wavelengths (350–450 nm) was displayed in Fig. 4B. Obviously, the emission wavelength of B,N-CDs showed the excitation-dependent characteristics. The maximum FL intensity was scored at 365 nm as the excitation wavelength ranged from 350 to 450 nm. Furthermore, as seen from the fluorescent 3D image in Fig. S2 (Supporting Information), the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths were measured to be 365 and 442 nm, respectively. Therefore, 365 nm were picked as the excitation wavelength in the following experiments.

The quantum yield of B,N-CDs was determined based on the fluorescent intensity to furtherly evaluate the FL properties. The QY of B,N-CDs was as high as 25.3% using quinine sulfate (QY = 54% at 365 nm) dissolved in H2SO4 (0.1 M, refractive index 1.33) as reference[32,33]. It is superior to a host of relevant reports (Table S2). The high QY was possibly attributed two factors. On the one hand, the surface state (surface modification or passivation) has a greater influence on the QY of CDs. In this study, boron can be used as an engine for charge transfer, especially in excited states [34,35]. Nitrogen can stabilize excitons in CDs and effectively passivate surface active sites, increasing the electron cloud density on the surface of CDs [36]. The synergy of N and B promoted the photoluminescent properties. On the other hand, nitrogen/oxygen-containing groups can also enhance the QY [36]. The doping of N produced a great deal of amino groups. Moreover, folic acid as a raw material in the synthesis process, which was rich in N and O, can introduce more nitrogen-containing functional groups. Thus, the high QY of B,N-CDs were acquired due to the doped nitrogen and/or boron atoms. In general, high quantum yield means higher performance [37].

Fluorescence lifetime was performed on FLS980 Series of Fluorescence Spectrometers and the result is shown in Fig. 4C. Notably, the decay curve was perfectly conformed to the bi-exponential decay kinetics. The faster component lifetime is founded to be 1.17 ns with the contribution of 10%, and the slower component lifetime is 8.33 ns with the contribution of 90% (Fig. S3 in Supporting Information). The average decay time of B,N-CDs was calculated to be 7.62 ns. The faster lifetime come from the conjugated core of CDs, and the slower lifetime was because of the surface trap states [38]. In the present system, the major contribution to the optical behavior originated from the surface states of B,N-CDs. Furthermore, B,N-CDs had excellent salt stability, pH stability, photobleaching stability, placement stability and fast quick response time towards Cu2+ (Fig. S4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on fluorescence assay system
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH towards the B,N-CDs in assaying Cu2+ was evaluated and the results were shown in Fig. S4B. Apparently, the addition of Cu2+ leads to the quenching of B,N-CDs FL intensity over the full range of pH from 3 to 12, especially showing a higher degree quenching in the range of 5–9. Thus, the pH range of 5–9 was the best range for Cu2+ assay. The possible reasons are described as follows: FL quenching efficiency decreased in highly acidic medium may be due to the protonation of B,N-CDs surface functional groups such as –NH2. A large amount of –NH2 were present on the surface of B,N-CDs due to the doping of the N element. The amino group on the surface of B,N-CDs will be protonized by H+, making it positively charged on the surface, thus increasing the repulsion towards copper ions, reducing the FL quenching effect. Simultaneously, in alkaline solution (pH greater than 8), due to the hydrolysis of Cu2+ to form Cu(OH)2 in alkaline media, making it hard to form a complex with B,N-CDs. Thus, the Cu2+ had little influence on FL intensity of B,N-CDs [31].

In order to evaluate the analytical performance of B,N-CDs for assaying of Cu2+, first, the feasibility of B,N-CDs towards Cu2+ was explored and the results were displayed in Fig. 5 A. Apparently, it is seen from Fig. 5A that B,N-CDs in the absence of Cu2+ exhibited strong fluorescent intensity. On the contrary, the fluorescent intensity of B,N-CDs with the present of Cu2+ was dramatically decreased. Fig. 5A inset displayed the photos of B,N-CDs in the absence (left) and present (right) of Cu2+, the solution has almost no FL when Cu2+ are present, and the FL was very intense in the absence of Cu2+. This is consistent with the results in Fig. 5A. These aforementioned analytical results indicate that Cu2+ can quench the FL of B,N-CDs effectively. Subsequently, the different concentrations Cu2+ (0–10−3 M) were added into B,N-CD solutions, respectively. The FL responses of above solutions were recorded and the results were displayed in Fig. 5B. As can be observed that the FL intensity of B,N-CDs decreased gradually as the concentrations of Cu2+ increased, indicating that Cu2+ has an obviously effect towards B,N-CDs. To acquire the quenching is static or dynamic, the Stern-Volmer relationship (F0/F = KSV[Q] + 1) was used, where F0 and F represent the FL intensity of B,N-CDs at 442 nm in the absence/presence of Cu2+, respectively, KSV is the Stern-Volmer’s constant and [Q] is the concentration of the quencher Cu2+. According to the Stern-Volmer relationship, the plot of F0/F as a function of [Q] should be linear. However, the plot of F0/F versus [Q] exhibited deviations from linearity (Fig. 5C), manifesting the presence of both static and dynamic quenching. To obtained the limit of detection (LOD) and the detection range, a modified Stern-Volmer equation F0/F = KSVlog[Q] + C) was used to get calibration plot, where C is constant. A good calibration plots of F0/F versus log[Q] was shown in Fig. 5D. Apparently, F0/F exhibited a nice linear relationship versus log[Q] when the concentration of Cu2+ range from 0.005 to 5 μM (R2 = 0.9970). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be around 1.6 nM (3 s/k). This was lower than that of numerous other fluorescent probes for assaying Cu2+ (Table S3). The possible mechanism are as follows: the amino group on the surface of B,N-CDs complex with Cu2+ to split the d orbital of Cu2+, causing the excited state electrons from B,N-CDs transfer to the d orbital of Cu2+. Thus, the radiation transition of the excited state electrons from B,N-CDs was squeezed, and the FL was quenched [23,24].

The selectivity of B,N-CDs in Cu2+ assay was also investigated. The influence of the metal ions (including Cu2+, Na+, K+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Cr3+, Pb2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Hg2+) towards B,N-CDs were examined. It is demonstrated in Fig. 6 A and Fig. S5 (Supporting Information) that, the value of F/F0 reduce considerably by adding Cu2+, while it keeps unchanged when other metal ions are added. Only Cu2+ quenches the FL of B,N-CDs significantly. By contrary, other metal ions hardly have quenching effect towards B,N-CDs. The results imply that B,N-CDs possess high selectivity for assaying Cu2+.

For purpose of assessing the anti-interference ability of the B,N-CDs, several metal cations (including Na+, Ni2+, K+, Cr3+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Al3+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Hg2+, Fe2+) commonly existed in water samples were tested. Different metal ions were mixed with B,N-CDs in the presence of Cu2+ respectively. Apparently, the other metal ions show no obvious influence on the FL of B,N-CDs in the presence of Cu2+ (Fig. 6B). The aforementioned results reveal that B,N-CDs maintain anti-interference ability when assaying Cu2+.

Based on the aforementioned analysis, B,N-CDs as the fluorescence probe exhibits a wide linear range 0.005–5 μM with a detection limit of 1.6 nM, satisfactory stability, selectivity and anti-interference performance, which is superior to the previous works in literatures.

To evaluate the application ability of B,N-CDs, it was served as fluorescent sensing platform in assaying Cu2+ in different real samples (waste water and serum). The waste water and serum samples come from Licun River (Qingdao, China) and the Qingdao center medical group, respectively. The recovery experiments were performed using a standard addition method. In addition, to compare with a well-established procedure, the spiked copper ions in the waste water and serum samples were detected by ICP-MS. The results are shown in Table S4. Apparently, the sample recoveries are in the range of 96.0–103.0% in waste water, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) is less than 2.1%. The concentrations of spiked Cu2+ in the waste water measured by the proposed method were about 98.0 to 106.1% as compared to those measured by ICP-MS method. For serum, the recoveries are estimated to be 98.0–101.0% together with the RSD varied from 0.9 to 1.5% and the concentrations of spiked Cu2+ in serum samples measured by the proposed method were about 100.0 to 102.2% as compared to those measured by ICP-MS method. The results indicated that this probe has high accuracy, precision and great potential in monitoring the amount of Cu2+ in waste water and serum fields.

Considering the utility of B,N-CDs as fluorescent probes for cell imaging, it is necessary to explore their biocompatibility and toxicity towards cells. The biocompatibility and toxicity of B,N-CDs was measured through MTT method using Hela cells. The optical density (OD) was obtained at 490 and 570 nm, respectively, and the results were shown in Table S5 (Supporting Information). The cell viability was obtained according to the Eq. (2). As shown in Fig. S6 (Supporting Information), the cell viability is up to 97% after cultivating for even 24 h with B,N-CDs (1/10). The results indicated that B,N-CDs possessed satisfying biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity towards Hela cells, implying the promising application in biological field.

Due to the satisfying low cytotoxicity and biocompatibility, cell imaging experiments were performed to further prove their feasibility in biological applications. Fig. 7 A represented the bright field image, and it can be seen from Fig. 7A that the cells were evenly distributed. Fig. 7B-C showed the dark field images, and the cells exhibited strong bright blue and green fluorescent under 405 nm and 488 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively, indicating that B,N-CDs successfully and efficiently entered the cells through endocytosis. In addition, compared with Fig. 7A, the cell morphology had no significant changes, further suggesting the cytotoxicity of B,N-CDs was negligible. Furthermore, the FL intensity of labeled Hela cells remained stable after constant excitation 1 h, indicating B,N-CDs possessed good photostability and low photobleaching. In view of this, the aforementioned results proved that B,N-CDs do have desirable biological application prospects.

B,N-CDs with a high QY (25.3%) had been successfully synthesized via one-pot hydrothermal method by using folic acid, glycerol and borax as raw materials. B,N-CDs exhibited the excitation-unrelated emission fluorescent and strong blue fluorescence in water. B,N-CDs were remarkably stable against wide pH, ionic strength, photobleaching and placement. Due to the fluorescence of B,N-CDs were selectively quenched by Cu2+, B,N-CDs were applied as a novel fluorescent probe for assaying Cu2+ in aqueous solution and real samples. Beyond that, B,N-CDs were also proved to be a non-toxic probe for multicolor imaging of Hela cells, which demonstrating that B,N-CDs had potential value for biological and biomedical research. Hence, B,N-CDs can be utilized as the promising probes for rapid Cu2+ assaying and cell imaging.

Alan Meng: Writing – review & editing. Baihe Huangfu: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Liying Sheng: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xiaocheng Hong: Data curation. Zhenjiang Li: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work reported here was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 52072196, 52002199, 52002200, Major Basic Research Program of Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2020ZD09, the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province under Grant No. ZR2019BEM042, the Innovation and Technology Program of Shandong Province under Grant No. 2020KJA004, the Open Project of the Chemistry Department in Qingdao University of Science and Technology of China (Grant No. QUSTHX201813), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2020 M683450) and the Taishan Scholars Program of Shandong Province under No. ts201511034. We express our grateful thanks to them for their financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106981.
